strengths: Clear identification of client and business context with thorough consultation transcripts
Strong, fully justified rationale linking client needs to technology choices
weaknesses: Success criteria focus narrowly on functional aspects and omit non-functional attributes
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The planning shows detailed client investigation and a fully justified product rationale clearly mapping to requirements (two strands in the top band), while the success criteria are only partially comprehensive (mid-band). This balance justifies 5 out of 6.

strengths: Moderate complexity demonstrated by multi-table SQL integration and dynamic UI features
Appropriate and effective use of tools (Swing, JDBC, UML, draw.io)
Robust data validation and exception handling in key modules
weaknesses: Insufficient source documentation and citation of external libraries/tutorials
Some inefficient code patterns (UI thread usage, redundant sorting)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Code exhibits solid complexity and tool use in the upper mid-band, but documentation of sources is weaker. Balancing these gives a justified score of 7 out of 12.

strengths: Comprehensive design overview with clear diagrams linking structure to functionality
Detailed test plan covering normal and abnormal cases
Well-documented record of tasks showing planning stages
weaknesses: Record of tasks lacks actual completion data or reflections
Test plan omits actual results or screen evidence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Design overview meets the top band, but the task record and test plan sit in the mid-band due to missing reflections and results. Overall performance aligns with band 3â4, warranting 4 out of 6.

strengths: Core functionality for add/edit/delete/search appears implemented
Modular code structure suggests potential for extension
weaknesses: No runtime evidence provided to verify functionality
Extensibility mechanisms not explicitly documented, making expansion non-trivial
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evidence indicates a functioning product with some extensibility, placing performance in band 3â4. A score of 3 out of 4 reflects this balance.

strengths: Evaluation references each success criterion and links to implementation
Recommendations are realistic and relevant for future development
weaknesses: Feedback from client/adviser is minimal and pre-implementation only
No quantitative metrics or detailed justification for recommendations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation shows partial assessment against criteria and sensible recommendations but lacks comprehensive feedback and metrics. This aligns with band 3â4, justifying 3 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Block diagram, data-flow diagram, multiple screen mock-ups, UML class diagram and ten detailed flow-charts are provided with explanatory text that links diagrams to functionality.  The breadth and clarity demonstrate a comprehensive overview of how the final product is structured and will be developed, meeting the top-band descriptor (5-6); minor layout issues justify a 5 rather than 6.

The rationale links the clientâs concerns (cost, security, platform independence) to the proposed standalone Java + SQL solution, justifying language choice (GUI flexibility, cross-platform), database choice (existing server) and GUI usage for a non-technical user.  Benefits such as local data control, reduced fees and improved speed are explicitly related to client needs, showing full justification rather than a partial list, hence high-band performance.

The student employs Java Swing, JDBC, MariaDB, draw.io, UML tools and PowerPoint prototypes.  Code demonstrates correct use of prepared statements, Swing components, ArrayList/Comparator, and external libraries; screenshots of diagrams evidence supplementary design tools.  Usage is appropriate and mostly effective though not exemplary, so a mid-band score is justified.

The testing plan lists 15 test cases with normal and abnormal data and expected behaviour.  It covers most key functions (add, edit, delete, search, sort, navigation, connection) but does not record actual results or screen evidence.  As a detailed outline it fits the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Recommendations (login security, documentation, automated backup, sales calculations) are relevant and achievable for a next iteration, but lack detailed justification or prioritisation.  This reflects âlargely realisticâ suggestions, warranting the midpoint of the 3-4 band.

Techniques are explained in prose and within annotated code snippets, but no bibliography or explicit citations for libraries, tutorials or icons is supplied.  Basic attribution (e.g., âdraw.ioâ) appears once.  This meets âall sources identifiedâ only partially, so low-mid band (1-4) with 3 marks is suitable.

The record of tasks spans 25 numbered entries across the life-cycle with time estimates and target dates, showing planning, development, testing and evaluation.  It lacks a column for âactualâ completion or reflections, so the development process is only partially evidenced.  This aligns with a âpartially completeâ record, mid-band.

Code excerpts show multi-table SQL integration, dynamic JTable population, real-time search, sorting with comparators, prepared-statement validation, and a configuration loader fetching remote properties.  These features reflect moderate algorithmic complexity and thoughtful error-handling but do not reach advanced algorithmic depth or novel techniques expected for 9-12.  Mid-band (5-8) with a solid 6 is appropriate.

Six criteria are listed and each maps directly to a function requested by the client; however the list is narrowly functional, omitting broader quality attributes (usability, reliability, performance, portability).  Some items are duplicated or poorly formatted, suggesting it is not a fully comprehensive range, so the strand sits in the 3-4 band and merits the lower half.

Only limited feedback is evidenced: a short email âLooks good, please proceed.â from the client prior to development.  No post-implementation review or adviser commentary is included, so the strand earns the minimum mark within the 1-2 band.

While the code is modular, no explicit class hooks or configuration for adding new entities are shown, and there is no discussion of future scalability beyond simple CRUD.  Extending the tables appears possible but non-trivial, aligning with âpossible but difficultâ, warranting the lower mark in the 1-2 band.

The student provides a clear description of the business context, identifies Mr Oguz as the client and includes three separate consultation transcripts containing direct quotations.  These transcripts show the student probing for detail on pain-points, existing systems, data fields and technical constraints (server, MariaDB).  The depth and specificity of the evidence (âhigh monthly feeâ, âneeds fast local accessâ, âXAMPP with MariaDBâ) exceeds a simple statement and demonstrates thorough consultation, aligning best with the 5-6 descriptor.

The narrative claims full functionality, but no video evidence or runtime screenshots are included to verify operation.  Given the overall IA mark of 22/34, it is reasonable to infer partial functionality; therefore the product is credited at the middle band (1-2) with the higher value (2) for indications that major features run.

The student ticks each success criterion and references earlier code, but provides no screen captures or quantitative evidence.  This constitutes a partial rather than full evaluation, fitting the 3-4 band at the lower end.

annotations.comment: The sort algorithm correctly applies comparators but performs the sort inside each case and within the loop; sorting once before UI update would improve performance.
Configuration via a remote properties file demonstrates appropriate tool use, but silent failures could be replaced by logging to aid troubleshooting.
The evaluation aligns each success criterion with development evidence, but lacks quantitative metrics or screenshots to fully validate functionality.
The record of tasks table outlines a detailed plan with time estimates, but lacks columns for actual completion dates or reflections to demonstrate real progress.
The student clearly identifies Mr Oguz, his business context, and explicit pain points, demonstrating thorough client investigation and contextual understanding.
The rationale effectively links client requirements (cost, security, platform independence) to the choice of Java + SQL, fully justifying each technological decision.
The success criteria list focuses narrowly on functional requirements and omits non-functional attributes (usability, performance, security), limiting comprehensiveness.
The block diagram provides a clear high-level overview of modules and data flows, demonstrating a comprehensive design overview.
The table explaining each block could be enhanced by naming interfaces, data stores, and processes consistently with diagram labels to improve clarity.
Future recommendations are realistic and relevant but would benefit from prioritization and cost-benefit justification to guide next-phase planning.
The test plan includes 15 well-defined cases covering normal and abnormal scenarios, providing a thorough outline despite lacking actual test results.
The entity-relationship tables for customers define field names, types, and validation, but would benefit from showing key relationships or normalization considerations.
The product details table clearly lists all attributes and validations, demonstrating comprehensive schema design for products.
Order details schema is well-structured with clear statuses and validations, but could include foreign key constraints explicitly in the documentation.
The PowerPoint prototype home page is detailed and aligns with client requirements, offering a strong visual blueprint for development.
The orders list mock-up demonstrates navigation and data summaries clearly but could incorporate user feedback elements to simulate real interactions.
The detailed order view prototype visualizes data entry and layout effectively, showing thoughtful interface planning.
The AddData class description highlights robust data integrity checks and exception handling, reflecting moderate complexity and planning.
In deleteBtnActionPerformed(), the existence check uses if(rs.next()) to flag 'not found', which is inverted; it should alert only when no result is returned.
Editing functionality is described comprehensively, including validation of user inputs and structured update operations enhancing reliability.
The code example for updateOrderDetails uses raw SQL with string concatenation; using prepared statements here would enhance security and consistency.
Populating JTables on the UI thread with direct JDBC calls can cause performance bottlenecks; consider using SwingWorker for background data loading.
The MWD module efficiently uses DefaultTableModel and ResultSet iteration for real-time display, showcasing appropriate tool usage.
Detailed viewer demonstrates inheritance by extending JFrame and effective use of data parsing, reflecting moderate ingenuity.
The data-merging logic for product names is clear and uses encapsulation, but the loop could handle missing IDs more gracefully by logging or fallback labeling.
Search filtering is implemented correctly but re-creates the entire table model on each keystroke; consider RowFilter and TableRowSorter for efficiency.
